edges.justification: Real yields represent the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding gold; when real yields rise, investors can earn a guaranteed real return in TIPS instead of gold, reducing gold demand and price.
The Fed Funds rate raises the return on cash and short-term instruments relative to non-yielding gold, directly affecting gold's opportunity cost and thus demand.
Rising market-implied inflation expectations increase demand for gold as an inflation hedge, pushing gold prices higher through the expectations channel.
Realized CPI inflation signals erosion of fiat-currency purchasing power, triggering inflation-hedge demand for gold and supporting higher prices.
Gold is priced in U.S. dollars globally; a stronger dollar makes gold more expensive for foreign buyers, reducing demand, while a weaker dollar boosts foreign purchasing power and gold demand.
Via risk-on/risk-off dynamics, strong equity returns draw capital away from defensive gold holdings, while equity sell-offs trigger safe-haven rotation into gold.
Elevated VIX signals heightened financial-market stress and risk aversion, driving safe-haven demand for gold as investors seek assets uncorrelated with equity drawdowns.
Widening investment-grade credit spreads signal systemic financial stress, triggering safe-haven demand for gold as investors rotate out of credit-exposed assets.
A rising debt-to-GDP ratio raises the perceived probability of currency debasement or fiscal repression, increasing demand for gold as a sovereign-credit hedge with no counterparty risk.
Oil price increases raise energy-intensive gold mining costs (supporting a price floor) and signal inflationary pressures that boost gold's inflation-hedge demand.
Global economic expansion (CLI above trend) rotates capital toward growth assets and away from defensive gold, while contraction signals boost safe-haven gold demand.
Higher mine production increases physical gold supply, exerting downward pressure on price; lower production constrains supply and supports higher prices, per standard supply-demand mechanics.
Net central-bank purchases add to physical gold demand directly, supporting higher prices; net selling increases available supply and pressures prices downward.
Positive ETF net inflows require physical gold purchases to back new shares, directly removing supply from the market and supporting prices; outflows release physical gold and pressure prices.

Jewelry consumption is the largest single category of physical gold demand; higher jewelry demand tightens physical supply and supports prices, while high prices inversely reduce price-sensitive consumer jewelry purchases.
Elevated geopolitical risk triggers flight-to-safety demand for gold as the primary tangible safe-haven asset, historically producing positive co-movement between GPR spikes and gold price increases.
High economic policy uncertainty raises the risk premium on policy-exposed assets and increases demand for gold as a counterparty-risk-free store of value unaffected by any single policy regime.

Greater consumer pessimism about multi-year economic conditions drives defensive retail demand for gold (bars, coins, ETFs), documented empirically as a statistically significant driver of real gold prices.

Silver and gold share overlapping inflation-hedge and safe-haven demand drivers, so common shocks (inflation expectations, financial stress, monetary debasement) move both prices, and arbitrage between the two precious metals creates direct price linkage.
Rising unemployment signals economic weakness, raising recession risk and safe-haven demand for gold, and also prompts Fed easing which lowers real yields and further supports gold.
The Fed Funds rate anchors the short end of the yield curve and influences longer-dated real yields through the expectations channel and term-structure transmission.
Changes in the Fed Funds rate signal shifts in monetary policy stance, which alter market expectations about future inflation and thus affect break-even inflation rates.

Real yields and break-even inflation together decompose the nominal 10-year Treasury yield by construction (nominal = real + breakeven), so changes in one component mechanically relate to the other given nominal yield dynamics.
Realized inflation updates investor beliefs about future inflation, causing market-implied break-even inflation expectations to adjust in the same direction.
The Federal Reserve's price-stability mandate means that rising realized inflation is a primary driver of Fed Funds rate increases, while declining inflation supports rate cuts.
Under the Fed's dual mandate, rising unemployment prompts monetary easing (lower Fed Funds), while falling unemployment toward full employment supports rate increases.
Higher U.S. policy rates relative to foreign rates attract capital inflows into dollar-denominated assets, strengthening the dollar (DXY), while rate cuts weaken it.
Higher U.S. real yields attract foreign capital seeking positive real returns in dollar assets, increasing demand for dollars and strengthening DXY.

Elevated equity-market volatility (VIX) typically coincides with increased perceived credit risk, causing credit spreads to widen as investors demand greater compensation for holding corporate debt during periods of financial stress.
VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and spikes during equity market sell-offs, creating a mechanical negative contemporaneous relationship with S&P 500 returns.
During risk-off episodes that elevate VIX, investors seek safety in U.S. dollar assets, strengthening the dollar (flight-to-dollar phenomenon alongside flight-to-gold).
Widening credit spreads signal deteriorating financial conditions that typically accompany equity market declines, as both reflect common risk-aversion shocks.

The OECD CLI anticipates global economic turning points; improving global growth expectations (rising CLI) support corporate earnings and equity returns, while deteriorating CLI signals weigh on equities.
Global economic activity is the primary driver of crude oil demand; above-trend global expansion (CLI > 100) increases industrial energy consumption and oil prices, while contraction reduces them.
The CLI leads turning points in economic activity; declining global economic momentum typically precedes rising unemployment as labor demand weakens.
Energy prices are a direct component of CPI and a major cost input across the economy; rising oil prices feed through to consumer-price inflation within months.
Oil price increases signal inflationary pressures and update investor inflation expectations, causing market-implied break-even inflation rates to rise.

A rising debt-to-GDP ratio raises concerns about future monetization or fiscal dominance, which can push long-term inflation expectations (break-even) higher as investors anticipate inflationary debt management.
Major geopolitical risk events create uncertainty that spills into financial markets, causing VIX to spike as investors bid up options protection against equity-market disruption.

Geopolitical risk events — particularly in major oil-producing regions — directly disrupt oil supply and create oil-price spikes; geopolitical uncertainty also creates precautionary demand in energy markets.

Geopolitical crises frequently trigger domestic economic policy responses (sanctions, trade restrictions, fiscal measures), causing EPU to rise alongside GPR during major international-conflict episodes.

Policy uncertainty about fiscal, monetary, and regulatory direction raises risk premia in equity markets, contributing to elevated implied volatility as investors hedge against policy-driven outcomes.
Policy uncertainty makes corporate cash-flow projections more volatile, increasing perceived credit risk and causing investors to demand wider spreads on corporate debt.

Consumer pessimism about multi-year economic prospects partly reflects and partly reinforces economic policy uncertainty; households perceiving uncertain or adverse policy directions report greater economic pessimism in surveys.
Consumer pessimism about five-year economic conditions signals broad deterioration in household confidence, which is associated with weaker consumer spending and equity returns.

Rising unemployment directly increases consumer pessimism about future economic conditions, as households facing job-loss risk or observing layoffs form more negative multi-year economic expectations.
Rising unemployment raises default risk for consumer and corporate borrowers (reduced revenues, weaker consumer demand), widening credit spreads as lenders demand greater risk compensation.

Both silver and oil are industrial commodities whose prices respond to global manufacturing activity and economic cycles; shared exposure to industrial demand creates co-movement, and energy is a cost input in silver mining and processing.

Gold ETF inflows tend to surge during high-VIX risk-off episodes as institutional investors seek safe-haven exposure; VIX is thus a driver of ETF flow decisions, while large ETF flows can signal prevailing market stress.

Falling real yields make gold-backed ETFs more attractive relative to TIPS, triggering ETF inflows; conversely, rising real yields prompt outflows as the opportunity cost of holding gold through ETFs increases.
Elevated geopolitical risk motivates central banks — particularly those in emerging markets concerned about sanctions or dollar-asset freezes — to accelerate strategic gold reserve accumulation.

Global consumer jewelry demand rises with economic prosperity (rising incomes in India, China, and other key markets); the OECD CLI's measure of global above-trend growth thus supports jewelry consumption.
Energy is a major variable cost in gold mining; higher oil prices raise mining operating costs, which can incentivize efficiency improvements or constrain output at marginal mines.

Higher sovereign debt levels raise concerns about fiscal sustainability and can push real term premia higher as investors demand additional compensation for holding long-duration U.S. government bonds.

Deteriorating global economic momentum (falling CLI) raises equity-market uncertainty and risk aversion, contributing to VIX elevation as investors anticipate weaker corporate earnings and economic stress.
